metadata.scope: First-time voter education for Indian general and state elections
metadata.disclaimer: This data reflects ECI rules as of the lastUpdated date. Always verify with eci.gov.in or the National Voters' Service Portal (NVSP) before acting.

entries.title: Basic eligibility to vote in India
entries.content: To vote in Indian elections, you must be: (1) a citizen of India, (2) at least 18 years old on the qualifying date (1st January of the year of revision of electoral roll), (3) ordinarily resident in the constituency where you wish to register, and (4) not disqualified under any law. There is no upper age limit.

entries.title: Who cannot vote
entries.content: You cannot vote if you are: (1) not a citizen of India, (2) below 18 years on the qualifying date, (3) declared of unsound mind by a competent court, (4) disqualified due to corrupt electoral practices, or (5) currently serving certain criminal sentences. Being in prison generally disqualifies a person from voting under Section 62(5) of the RP Act, with exceptions for those in preventive detention.

entries.title: How to register as a new voter (Form 6)
entries.content: To register as a new voter, fill Form 6. You can apply: (1) Online at voters.eci.gov.in or via the Voter Helpline App, or (2) Offline at your local Booth Level Officer (BLO) or Electoral Registration Officer (ERO) office. The process is FREE — no fees are charged. Required documents: proof of age (birth certificate, Class 10 marksheet, passport, PAN, Aadhaar, driving licence) and proof of ordinary residence (Aadhaar, passport, ration card, electricity/water/gas bill, bank passbook).

entries.title: When to register and how long it takes
entries.content: You can register any time after turning 17 — ECI now accepts advance applications from 17-year-olds, who get added to the rolls automatically when they turn 18. Processing typically takes 2-4 weeks after application. The electoral roll is revised annually with qualifying dates of 1st January, 1st April, 1st July, and 1st October. You can also register or update during Special Summary Revisions before elections.

entries.title: Voter ID card (EPIC)
entries.content: After successful registration, you receive an Elector Photo Identity Card (EPIC). It contains your photo, EPIC number, name, age, address, and constituency. EPIC is also available digitally as e-EPIC — a downloadable PDF from voters.eci.gov.in, valid as ID. You do NOT need a physical EPIC to vote — any of 12 alternative photo IDs accepted by ECI work, but you must be on the electoral roll.

entries.title: What ID to bring to vote
entries.content: You must carry one of these photo IDs to vote: (1) EPIC voter card, (2) Aadhaar card, (3) Passport, (4) Driving licence, (5) PAN card, (6) Service ID with photo (govt/PSU/public sector), (7) Bank/Post Office passbook with photo, (8) Smart card under NPR, (9) MGNREGA job card, (10) Health insurance smart card under Ministry of Labour, (11) Pension document with photo, (12) Official ID for MPs/MLAs/MLCs, or (13) Unique Disability ID (UDID). Even without EPIC, any of these will work IF your name is on the voter list.

entries.title: Step-by-step polling process
entries.content: On polling day: (1) Find your polling booth at voters.eci.gov.in or via SMS to 1950. (2) Reach during polling hours (usually 7 AM - 6 PM, varies by state). (3) First Polling Officer verifies your name on the electoral roll and checks your ID. (4) Second Polling Officer applies indelible ink on your left index finger and gives you a slip. (5) Third Polling Officer activates the EVM ballot. (6) Cast your vote by pressing the button next to your candidate. (7) Verify the VVPAT slip shows your choice for 7 seconds, then it drops into the sealed box.

entries.title: EVM and VVPAT explained
entries.content: Electronic Voting Machine (EVM) is a tamper-proof, standalone device (not connected to internet) used for voting since the 1990s. It has a Ballot Unit where you press a button next to your candidate and a Control Unit operated by the Polling Officer. VVPAT (Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail) is a printer attached to the EVM that prints a paper slip showing your vote choice — visible through a glass for 7 seconds, then automatically dropped into a sealed box for verification. ECI conducts random VVPAT counting in 5 polling stations per assembly segment.

entries.title: NOTA (None of the Above)
entries.content: NOTA is the last option on every EVM, allowing you to reject all candidates while still exercising your right to vote. Introduced in 2013 following a Supreme Court ruling, it ensures vote secrecy even for those rejecting all candidates. However, NOTA does not currently lead to re-election regardless of vote count — the candidate with the highest valid votes still wins.

entries.title: Finding your polling booth
entries.content: Find your polling booth by: (1) Visiting voters.eci.gov.in and clicking 'Search in Electoral Roll' — search by EPIC number, name, or mobile. (2) Using the Voter Helpline App (Android/iOS). (3) SMSing 'EPIC <your EPIC number>' to 1950. (4) Calling toll-free 1950 (with your local STD code). Your voter slip from the BLO before elections also lists the booth address.

entries.title: Correcting voter roll details (Form 8)
entries.content: Use Form 8 to: correct errors in your existing entry (name spelling, age, address within same constituency), update your photo, mark yourself as Person with Disability (PwD), or shift to a new address. Available online at voters.eci.gov.in. Form 8A was earlier used for transposition within a constituency but has been merged into Form 8 since August 2022.

entries.title: Voting while away from home
entries.content: If you'll be away from your registered constituency on polling day, your options are: (1) Travel back — there is no proxy voting for general voters. (2) Re-register at your new address using Form 8 (this removes you from the old roll). (3) Postal ballot — only available to specific categories: service voters (armed forces, paramilitary, govt employees on election duty), absentee voters above 80 years, persons with 40%+ disability, COVID-positive/suspect voters, and electors on essential services. NRIs currently must travel to India to vote in person at their registered constituency.

entries.title: Model Code of Conduct (MCC)
entries.content: The Model Code of Conduct is a set of guidelines issued by ECI that comes into force the moment elections are announced and stays until results are declared. It restricts the ruling party from using official position for campaigning, prohibits new policy announcements, regulates campaign conduct, bans hate speech, and ensures level playing field. Violations can result in censure, FIRs, or candidate disqualification.

entries.title: Your vote is secret
entries.content: Voting in India is by secret ballot — no one (not your family, employer, party worker, or election officer) can legally see or demand to know who you voted for. The polling booth has a privacy screen around the EVM. You are not required to disclose your vote, and asking under coercion is illegal. Your EPIC and electoral roll entry are public records, but your actual vote is not.

entries.title: Reporting violations (cVIGIL app)
entries.content: Report MCC violations or electoral malpractice via the cVIGIL app (Android/iOS). You can submit photo/video evidence; complaints are GPS-tagged and addressed within 100 minutes. Identity is kept confidential. Use it to report cash distribution, hate speech, illegal posters, transport of liquor, or threats to voters. You can also call 1950 or email complaint@eci.gov.in.

entries.title: First-time voter checklist
entries.content: If you're voting for the first time: (1) Confirm you turn 18 on or before the qualifying date. (2) Apply via Form 6 at voters.eci.gov.in — free, takes 10 minutes. (3) Upload age proof and address proof. (4) Track application status on the same portal using your reference number. (5) Once approved, download e-EPIC. (6) Before polling day, find your booth on voters.eci.gov.in. (7) On polling day, carry your EPIC or any approved photo ID. (8) Reach the booth, vote, verify VVPAT slip — done!

entries.title: Types of elections in India
entries.content: India has elections at multiple levels: (1) Lok Sabha (general elections) — every 5 years, electing MPs to the lower house of Parliament. (2) Rajya Sabha — indirectly elected by state MLAs. (3) Vidhan Sabha (state assembly) — every 5 years per state. (4) Vidhan Parishad — in 6 states only, indirect. (5) Local body elections — Panchayats (rural) and Municipal Corporations (urban), conducted by State Election Commissions. (6) Presidential and Vice-Presidential — by electoral college, not direct vote.

entries.title: Your rights as a voter
entries.content: As a registered voter you have the right to: (1) Vote freely without coercion. (2) A secret ballot. (3) Information about candidates' criminal antecedents, assets, and education (mandatory affidavit on ECI website). (4) Vote-by-mail if you qualify (see absentee voting). (5) Demand assistance at the booth if you're a PwD voter. (6) Lodge complaints via cVIGIL or 1950. (7) Use NOTA. (8) Verify your VVPAT slip. Employers must legally grant a paid holiday on polling day under Section 135B of the RP Act.
